titleStmt.title: Letter from John P. Boland to Inspector General R.I.C., 11 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: John Pius Boland

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
This letter was sent by John Pius Boland (1870 – 1958). Boland was an Irish nationalist politician and lawyer. Like the other members of the Irish Party, Boland was taken completely by surprise by the Easter Rising in 1916 but continued to hope that an agreement could be forged by the various political groupings in Ireland.
The letter was sent to the inspector general of the Royal Irish constabulary (RIC). Up until July of 1916 this position had been occupied by Sir Neville Francis Fitzgerald Chamberlain (1856 – 1944). Chamberlain held the position from 1900 until his resignation in July 1916, making him the second longest serving inspector general in the force's history. He was succeeded by Sir John Aloysius Byrne (1874 -1942). Byrne was a soldier and administrator. In Dublin, during and after the 1916 rising, he held the rank of brigadier-general in the Irish command while acting as deputy adjutant-general.
The letter was written by Boland as a query to the authority behind the policy of prohibiting the selling of certain postcards depicting the Rising in a local Kenmare shop. Enclosed with the letter is a report from the district inspector’s office stating that a prosecution would not be judicious as Kenmare has been very quiet during and since the Rising. The note also states that Norah Casey was anti-British before the Rising and therefore can now be looked at as a Sinn Féin sympathiser. There is also another enclosure of a statement describing the event and the republican photographs which Casey was advertising in her shop and an amendment allowing for the ordinary display of portraits unaccompanied by Sinn Féin badges or cards or documents containing 'last words' of rebel leaders.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
July 11. 1916. Dear Sir I have been informed by a Kenmare
shop keeper that the local police have warned
her against selling certain postcards picturing
the late rising. I should be glad to learn if this action
is the result of directions from headquarters or
is purely directed locally. I may add that I have not seen the postcards,
and that I express no opinions in consequence
as to the wisdom or otherwise of the
police action. Yrs truly
John P. Boland
The Inspector Gen.
R.I.C.
Display of Rebel Photographs
in Kenmare COUNTY OF Kerry
DISTRICT INSPECTORS OFFICE
Kenmare 4.7.1916 I beg to submit copy of State-
ment sent to Garrison Com-
mander Queenstown. In sub-
mitting it I said -
I think a prosecution would
not be judicious. The town
of Kenmare has been very
quiet during & since the
rebellion, & I attribute the
putting up the photographs
again to the advice probably
of some Sinn Feiner who would
like to gain some notoriety
for Kenmare. Norah Casey
has been anti-Btitish before
the rebellion & may now be
looked on as a Sinn Fein
Sympathiser The Inspector General
C.I.
Report result
W A O'Connell
DIT
5/7/16
I Office acknowledge
receipt & say that
inquiries are being made
II C.I.
Were these ordinary
photographs of the rebels
By what special orders
did the Sergeant take action
W A O'Connell
DIT
12.7.16
Done
13/7
County Inspector's Office,
Tralee. 7th day of July 1916
Transmitted. ID.I. for
C.I. on Insp 2/11
F A. Britten
D.I. Kenmare 8.7.16 I beg to state the C.M.A has
informed me that no police
action is to be taken. The County Inspector
County Inspector's Office,
Tralee.
10th day of July 1916
Submitted.
Hugh ONeill
C.I.
I.G.
County Inspector's Office,
Tralee. 14th day of July 1916
Transmitted.
F A Britten
D.I. Kenmare I.D.I. for
C.I. on duty
Sinn Fein Photographs displayed in Shop Window.
Kenmare 4:7:16. I beg to report that on Saturday 1st that I saw
a number of Photos of Sinn Fein leaders displayed in the shop
window of Miss Norah Casey of Henry St. Kenmare. I entered the
shop and told Miss Casey that there was a military order prohibiting
the displaying of those Photographs in shop windows and I
warned her to remove those she had displayed. She replied
if it is an order I suppose I must obey it, but how am I
to sell the Photos if people don't know I have them? I
said that whatever about the selling, she could not have
them displayed in the window and that to avoid trouble
she had better remove them. She then removed the
Photos displayed. When on patrol last night I again saw the following
Photos displayed in her shop window viz :- Finian Lynch, B.A.;
Sean MacDiarmada; Michael O'Halloran; E, Daly;
Cornelius Colbert; J.J.Walsh T.C. Cork; Countess Markievicz;
Sheehy Skeffington & P.H. Pearse. Each Photo was headed
Irish Rebellion May 1916 and underneath gave the name
of the Photo displayed. On passing Miss Casey's shop this morning I saw
some of the Photos still displayed in the window. R. Harvey Sgt 56188
Dist Inspr.
